obligations.reason: The AES wrapper exposes the entire 192-bit key (key_in → key_big) as readable via the external bus interface at multiple address offsets. There is no visible privilege check, lock bit, or access-control logic in the visible assignments. This could allow any bus master to extract the cryptographic key.
obligations.subject: Any bus master (CPU, peripherals, debug)
obligations.expected_guard: Privilege level check, lock bit, or debug authentication state
obligations.candidate_path: external_bus_io.rdata assignment selecting key_big[191:160] … key_big[31:0] depending on address offset
required_evidence_slots: RTL code for address decoding of external_bus_io to key slices
Any write-protection or lock logic on the key register
System-level address map and privilege signalling for AES peripheral
obligations.uncertainty: Whether the key is readable only after authentication is unclear from these fragments.

obligations.reason: The AES wrapper exposes intermediate AES state (inter_state) via the same external bus read path. Like the key, this can leak sensitive cryptographic state to any bus master that can drive the read interface.
obligations.subject: Any bus master (CPU, peripherals, debug)
obligations.expected_guard: Privilege check or lock to prevent observation of ongoing computation
required_evidence_slots: RTL for external_bus_io read mux
Address decode for inter_state slave offsets
Any side-effect of reading intermediate state
obligations.uncertainty: Exposure path is confirmed by the listed assignments; guard analysis requires full module.

obligations.reason: Both bootrom and rom2 have write-enable inputs (we_i) and write data paths (wdata_i) but only read assignments are visible. It is unclear whether spurious writes can corrupt the ROM content, which stores boot code and possibly security-sensitive constants.
obligations.subject: Any bus master (CPU, debug, DMA)
obligations.expected_guard: Hardware-based write protection (e.g., mask we_i with a fixed tie to 0, or address-space protection)
obligations.candidate_path: we_i → internal memory write logic (not visible); address decode to select write target
state_conditions_to_check: we_i logic used in memory block
required_evidence_slots: Full RTL of bootrom.sv and rom2.sv (especially always_ff blocks handling writes)
Evidence that we_i is ignored or that we_i is tied to 0 at higher level
obligations.uncertainty: Write vulnerability exists only if we_i is actually used in internal write logic.

obligations.reason: The debug infrastructure (SimJTAG, dmi_jtag, SimDTM, debug_req) connects to an AXI master (i_axi_master_dm) and an AXI slave. This debug path may be able to issue arbitrary read/write transactions without undergoing the same privilege checks as the CPU. If unauthenticated, this fully bypasses all access-control mechanisms.
obligations.subject: Debug AXI master (via JTAG/DMI)
obligations.object: All peripherals (AES, ROM, PLIC, UART, SPI, Ethernet)
obligations.expected_guard: Debug authentication / lock state (e.g., secure debug module, sticky unlock sequence)
obligations.candidate_path: JTAG → dmi_jtag → debug_req → core debug unit → AXI master → system bus → target slave
state_conditions_to_check: Debug authentication state (lock bit, key match)
required_evidence_slots: Connection of i_axi_master_dm to main memory/interconnect
Debug module RTL showing authorization before enabling AXI transactions
Lifecycle state machine that controls debug unlock
obligations.uncertainty: Need to confirm debug module capabilities; some RISC-V debug specs require authorization.

obligations.reason: A test_en_i signal is present in axi_node_intf_wrap and a test_en signal in ariane_testharness. Test modes often disable security checks or expose internal state. Without evidence that test_en is either statically disabled or does not weaken access controls, it may be a backdoor.
obligations.subject: Test interface (via test_en)
obligations.object: Access-control logic, debug locks, internal state visibility
obligations.expected_guard: Test enable must be irrevocably tied to inactive state in production, or must not relax any security property
obligations.candidate_path: test_en_i → logic that gates protection (e.g., bypass privilege check in node)
state_conditions_to_check: test_en_i value at reset and during normal operation
Usage inside axi_node_intf_wrap to alter routing or bypass checks
required_evidence_slots: RTL showing how test_en_i affects privilege or access decisions in axi_node_intf_wrap
External pin/register driving test_en in testharness
obligations.uncertainty: test_en_i may be harmless if only used for DFT and not affecting security gating.

obligations.reason: The ariane_peripherals module outputs a jtag_key[31:0] that likely participates in debug unlock. If this key register is writable by software without a write-once or lock mechanism, an attacker could reprogram it to a known value and gain unauthorized debug access.
obligations.subject: Any bus master with write access to the jtag_key register
obligations.expected_guard: Lock bit or write-once semantics after first write, or write restricted to secure boot phase
obligations.candidate_path: APB bus → ariane_peripherals → jtag_key output register
state_conditions_to_check: Write strobe gating after initial write
required_evidence_slots: RTL inside ariane_peripherals that implements jtag_key write path
Any lock signal associated with jtag_key
Documentation of debug unlock protocol
obligations.uncertainty: The real security relies on whether the unlock mechanism uses a non-reprogrammable secret or a sticky lock.

obligations.reason: The axi_node_intf_wrap receives priv_lvl_i (privilege level) and uses it in connectivity_mapping. If the interconnect does not correctly gate access based on privilege for security-critical slaves (like AES, ROM), machine-mode-only assets could be accessible from user-mode code.
obligations.subject: User-mode bus master (CPU) or any master not asserting machine privilege
obligations.object: Configuration registers or memory of AES, ROM, or other protected slaves
obligations.expected_guard: Address-range-based privilege check: access to certain addresses must require machine mode privilege
obligations.candidate_path: CPU → AXI master → axi_node_intf_wrap → destination slave, with priv_lvl_i used to block non-machine transactions
state_conditions_to_check: Connectivity mapping rules for each slave port
required_evidence_slots: RTL implementing connectivity_mapping and how priv_lvl_i is factored into slave selection/gating
Address map assigning privileges to peripherals
obligations.uncertainty: It is unknown whether the interconnect enforces privilege or passes it downstream for slave-side checks.

obligations.reason: Reset conditions for AES internal state (inter_state, ct, key registers) are not visible in the provided assignments. If these registers retain data after a system reset, a subsequent boot stage could read leftover cryptographic material, violating residue security.
obligations.subject: Reset controller / system reset
obligations.object: AES internal state and key registers (inter_state, ct, key_big)
obligations.expected_guard: All security-critical state must be cleared to known values (e.g., 0) on reset
obligations.candidate_path: rst_ni → AES wrapper registers: inter_state, ct, key_big
state_conditions_to_check: Reset value assignments in always_ff blocks for inter_state, ct, key_big
required_evidence_slots: RTL for the always_ff blocks describing reset behavior of inter_state, ct, and key_big
Any explicit or implicit power-on-reset values
obligations.uncertainty: Lack of visibility does not mean they are not cleared; verification needed.
